title: Polish potato pancakes
subtitle: Grated potato, onion, egg, flour. Fried crisp; served with sour cream.
excerpt: Placki ziemniaczane: thin pancakes of grated raw potato bound with onion, egg, and a spoon of flour, fried until the edges are lacy and brown. Served with a heavy dollop of cold sour cream and a scatter of chives. Five ingredients and the rule of squeezing the potato dry.
sourceNotes: Placki ziemniaczane are the Polish form of the central European potato pancake (German Reibekuchen, Czech bramboráky, Belarusian draniki, Yiddish latkes). All descend from the same nineteenth-century working kitchen and all use the same method: grated raw potato squeezed dry, bound minimally, fried in a hot pan. The Polish version is traditionally thin and lacy at the edges; the Belarusian draniki sit thicker and tighter. They are eaten plain, with sour cream, with apple sauce, or topped with a stew (gulasz placki).

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked pancakes freeze well between sheets of baking paper. Reheat in a hot oven (200°C) for ten minutes; they crisp back up. Raw batter does not keep; the potato oxidises and turns grey within an hour.
recipe.batchNotes: Frying is the rate-limiting step; the batter must go in the pan as soon as it is made or the potato turns. Do not batch the batter; do batch the frying across two pans.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best fried straight away. If holding for the table, keep finished pancakes warm on a rack in a 100°C oven; do not stack or they steam soft.

content.text: Floury potatoes are best: Maris Piper, King Edward, or any baking variety. Waxy potatoes give a tighter, less lacy pancake. Grate them coarsely on a box grater, not in a food processor (which makes the pieces too uniform and the texture pasty). Squeeze the grated potato dry in a clean tea towel — really dry, with weight behind it. Wet potato fries to slabs of starchy mush; dry potato fries to lacy gold.

The egg holds the pancake together; the flour catches the water that comes out of the potato as it cooks. Onion grated through the same grater gives the pancake its taste. The batter must go in the pan as soon as it is mixed, before the potato oxidises and the colour goes grey.

items.prepNote: around 800 g total, floury variety, peeled

content.text: Grate the {{potato}} of peeled potatoes coarsely into a large bowl. Grate the {{onion}} of onion through the same holes; the onion juice goes into the potato and slows oxidation. Tip everything into a clean tea towel or muslin cloth, gather the corners, and twist firmly over the sink. Squeeze until no more liquid comes out; expect a surprising amount. Let the squeezed cloud of starch settle at the bottom of the bowl you grated into (it will look milky) and pour off the clear liquid on top, then scrape the starch back into the dried potato. The starch helps the pancakes hold together.

Tip the dry potato and onion back into the large bowl. Add the {{eggs}} beaten eggs, the {{plain-flour}} of flour, a generous pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper. Mix until just combined; the texture is shaggy, not smooth. The mixture should hold together loosely on a spoon but still feel light.

Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into a 30 cm frying pan over medium-high heat, about 5 mm deep. When the oil shimmers, drop heaped tablespoons of batter into the pan and flatten each one with the back of the spoon to about 8 mm thick and 10 cm across. Work in batches of three or four; do not crowd. Fry for two and a half to three minutes a side until the edges are deeply lacy-gold and the centres are firm to a fingertip press. Lift onto a rack to drain. Repeat with the rest of the batter, topping up the oil between batches if needed.
Pile the warm pancakes onto a platter. Spoon over the {{soured-cream}} of cold soured cream and scatter the {{chives}} of snipped chives across. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Pancakes are pale and slimy in the middle
items.cause: Potato was not squeezed dry enough; batter is too wet.
items.fix: Squeeze the potato much harder than you think necessary. If the batter looks puddly on the spoon, return it to the cloth and squeeze again. A spoon of extra flour helps as a last resort.

items.symptom: Pancakes broke apart in the pan
items.cause: Not enough binder, or moved too soon.
items.fix: Make sure both eggs are in; flour is in. Wait until the underside has set firmly before turning; the pancake should slide on the spatula.

items.symptom: Outside burnt before the centre cooked
items.cause: Oil too hot, or pancakes pressed too thick.
items.fix: Drop the heat to medium. Press the pancakes thinner; aim for 8 mm. Thicker pancakes take longer than the edges can stand.

items.symptom: Batter turned grey while waiting for the pan
items.fix: Mix the batter only when the pan is ready and the first oil is shimmering. Cook the whole batch in succession; do not pause.

items.cause: Oil too cool, or pancakes drained on a flat plate.
items.fix: Make sure the oil is shimmering before each batch. Drain on a wire rack so the underside stays open and crisp.

content.text: Topped with a Hungarian-style beef gulasz, the pancakes become a full meal (gulasz placki). For the Jewish version (latkes), drop the onion grated through and add a teaspoon of baking powder for lift; serve with apple sauce alongside the sour cream. The Belarusian draniki version makes the pancakes thicker, plate-sized, and one per person. A grated carrot or a handful of grated cheese stirred through the batter is a small-child-friendly variation.

Placki ziemniaczane are the everyday lunch and the late-night supper of Polish kitchens. They cross every regional border in central and eastern Europe under different names: latkes in Jewish kitchens, draniki in Belarusian ones, bramboráky in Czech, Reibekuchen in German. The technique is the same; the regional difference is in thickness, what gets stirred through, and what goes on top. The cold soured cream is the universal partner.
